name: George Wallington
abstract: George Wallington (October 27, 1924 – February 15, 1993) was an American jazz pianist and composer. Born in Sicily, his career as a pianist began in the early 1940s, when he played with Dizzy Gillespie and Charlie Parker and contributed to the development of bebop.

about_quotes.speaker: Annie Ross
about_quotes.evidence: I knew George Wallington’s wife, who, you know he was the piano player with the Lionel Hampton band when I joined. He joined as well.
about_quotes.doc_title: Annie Ross interviewed by Monk Rowe, New York City, New York, January 13, 2001

about_quotes.speaker: Jackie McLean
about_quotes.evidence: Paul was working – he and I both were working with George Wallington’s band with A.T. [Arthur Taylor], in the Bohemia quartet.
about_quotes.doc_title: Jackie McLean — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

about_quotes.speaker: Art Farmer
about_quotes.evidence: we had our pianist named George Wallington, who a white guy, he came in the band
about_quotes.doc_title: Art Farmer — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

about_quotes.speaker: Sheila Jordan
about_quotes.evidence: The interviewer asks 'It was either George Wallington, or was it Al Haig?' and the interviewee responds 'Al Haig, Al Haig. Right, Al Haig.' George Wallington is not mentioned again by the interviewee, nor is any personal interaction described.
about_quotes.doc_title: Sheila Jordan — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

about_quotes.speaker: Monk Montgomery
about_quotes.evidence: The interviewee narrates that Lionel Hampton hired George Wallington as a replacement in his band, but the interviewee does not describe a personal interaction or direct professional relationship with Wallington himself.

about_quotes.speaker: Pete Jolly
about_quotes.evidence: Because I had gone through a lot of influences since then, it’s like a recipe, you know you take a little bit of Red Garland, a little bit or Horace Silver, a little bit of George Wallington...
about_quotes.doc_title: Pete Jolly interviewed by Monk Rowe, Los Angeles, California, February 15, 1999
